1: Bezalel made the ark (ha'aron) — 2.5 × 1.5 × 1.5 cubits — of acacia wood. Bezalel is named as the ark's maker in the execution account, the only craftsman specifically named for a specific object. His name means 'in the shadow of God,' and the object he personally made is the one over which the divine shadow rests — the ark covered by the mercy seat where YHWH's glory dwelt. The naming is deliberate: the Spirit-filled craftsman and the Spirit-indwelt object belong together in the narrative.
2: Gold overlay inside and out with a golden crown border. The execution replicates the blueprint exactly (Exodus 25:11). The inside of the ark — never seen by any human after the mercy seat was placed on it — was overlaid with gold, an act of workmanship that had no human audience. The inner gold was made for YHWH alone. This is perhaps the most striking expression in the tabernacle narrative of work done entirely coram Deo — in the sight of God without human witness.
3: Four gold rings cast for the ark's four feet, two per side. The rings were cast (yatzaq — to pour/cast metal) rather than hammered — indicating that the ring-forming process involved molten gold poured into molds. The casting technique produced the consistent ring geometry needed for the poles to slide in and out smoothly for transport.
4: Acacia-wood poles overlaid with gold for carrying the ark. The poles were made separate from the ark body and inserted into the rings. The carrying system's design — two men per pole, one pole per side — meant the ark could be transported by four Levites without any of them touching the ark itself. The poles mediated the gap between human hands and the sacred object.
5: The poles inserted in the rings on the two sides for carrying. The execution account confirms the ring-and-pole assembly was completed. The permanent nature of the poles (Exodus 25:15 — 'they shall not be taken from it') means the ark was never without its transport infrastructure. Even in the most holy place in the Jerusalem temple (1 Kings 8:8), the poles remained — visible reminders of YHWH's character as the God who accompanies his people on the move.
6: The mercy seat (kaporet) — 2.5 × 1.5 cubits of pure gold. The mercy seat was made separately and placed on top of the ark (Exodus 37:9). Pure gold, hammered to form a slab matching the ark's top dimensions exactly. The kaporet was the most expensive object in the tabernacle per unit area — solid gold throughout, no acacia core — because the place of atonement could bear no impure material at its center.
7: Two hammered-gold cherubim at the two ends of the mercy seat. 'Hammered' (mikshah) indicates the cherubim were formed by the metalsmith's hammer from a solid gold sheet or ingot — not cast. The beating-out technique required exceptional skill: the complex three-dimensional form of the cherubim (wings, faces, bodies) had to be shaped entirely by hand from a single piece of gold without seams or joins.
8: One cherub at each end, emerging from the mercy seat (mikanda*). The cherubim were formed integrally from the mercy seat material — the atonement cover and its guardian figures are one object. In ANE iconography, cherubim flanking a throne or sacred object were standard. YHWH's throne-room design placed the guardian figures not to the sides but as integral parts of the throne's surface, emerging from the place of atonement itself.
9: The cherubim spread their wings upward, shielding the mercy seat, their faces toward each other and toward the mercy seat. The posture precisely implements the blueprint (Exodus 25:20). The cherubim's downward gaze toward the mercy seat directs the viewer's attention to the same point — the place where blood was applied on Yom Kippur, the place where YHWH communicated with Moses, the place where the covenant demands and divine mercy converged.
10: The table (shulchan) — 2 × 1 × 1.5 cubits of acacia, gold-overlaid with a golden crown border. Bezalel (or his team) made the table according to Exodus 25:23–30. The table's proportions (Exodus 2:1:1.5) match the ark's width-to-length ratio but in a smaller scale. The crown border on the table, like those on the ark and incense altar, identifies it as a throne-room object.
11: Gold overlay and golden crown border, implementing Exodus 25:24. The table's gold overlay on all surfaces (including the bottom, unseen in use) followed the same principle as the ark: every surface was gold because the work was for YHWH, not for human display. The standard of workmanship was not calibrated to what worshipers would see but to what YHWH would inhabit.
12: The frame/molding (misgeret*) of a handsbreadth around the table with a golden crown on the frame. The practical function (retaining the showbread) was executed with the same decorative quality as all other table elements. In sacred architecture, the functional and the beautiful were never separated: every utilitarian element was crafted to the same standard as the purely decorative.
13: Four gold rings cast for the table, one per corner. The table's transport system mirrors the ark's (and the incense altar's and the bronze altar's): rings cast into the object's structure, poles inserted for Levitical carrying. The consistency of transport design across all sanctuary objects enabled the Levites to develop a single set of carrying protocols applicable to all objects.
14: The rings positioned close to the frame, to hold the carrying poles. The ring placement (near the frame rather than at the legs) meant the poles ran beneath the tabletop, keeping the table balanced during transport. The center-of-gravity engineering of the pole placement shows that the sanctuary's designers considered practical transport physics as part of the sacred design.
15: Acacia-wood poles overlaid with gold for carrying the table. Every set of carrying poles in the sanctuary was gold-overlaid — the transport infrastructure was as precious as the objects it carried. The poles were not utility tools that happened to serve sacred objects; they were sacred objects themselves, part of the sanctuary's total material economy.
16: The table's utensils — plates, dishes, jugs, and bowls for pouring libations — all of pure gold. The service vessels (Exodus 25:29) were made to specification. The full gold-utensil suite meant every interaction with the showbread (setting, removing, libating) was conducted with objects of the same material quality as the table itself. The service of the bread-of-presence was conducted entirely in gold.
17: The lampstand (menorah) of pure hammered gold (zahav tahor mikshah): shaft, base, branches, cups, calyxes, and petals all in one piece. The lampstand was the most technically demanding object in the entire sanctuary — hammered from a single mass of gold (Exodus 25:31) into a seven-branched form with seventeen decorative cup-calyx-petal units. No molds, no joins, no assembly: one piece of beaten gold shaped into botanical complexity.
18: Six branches from the lampstand's sides — three on each side. The six branches plus the central shaft = seven lamps. The seven-lamp design was not arbitrary: seven is the number of completeness/perfection in Hebrew symbolism (Genesis 2:2; Revelation 1:20), and the seven flames burning in the holy place represented the completeness of divine illumination in YHWH's dwelling.
19: Three almond-blossom cups per branch — calyx and petal — for each of the six branches. The detailed botanical vocabulary (gevia'im meshukadim — almond-shaped cups, kaphtor — calyx, perach — petal/flower) reflects precise design language. The almond-blossom motif connected the lampstand to themes of divine watchfulness and priestly legitimacy (cf. Aaron's almond-blossoming staff in Numbers 17:8, which confirmed Levitical election).
20: Four almond-blossom cups on the central shaft — one more than each branch — establishing the shaft's dominance in the design. The central shaft's extra cup meant it bore the greatest decorative elaboration, visually identifying it as the trunk from which the six branches grew. The arboreal design (trunk with branches, botanical decorations) made the lampstand a stylized tree — evoking Eden's tree of life in the sanctuary's most light-giving object.
21: One calyx under each pair of branches — three calyxes marking the three branching points along the shaft. The structural calyxes (different from the decorative cup-calyxes on the branches) served as visual brackets at each point where two branches met the shaft. They formalized the junction points, giving the lampstand a rhythmic visual structure from bottom to top.
22: The calyxes and branches were one piece with the shaft — all of pure gold. The unity of construction is reiterated (as in the blueprint, Exodus 25:36). The lampstand was an engineering marvel precisely because of this unity requirement: modern artisans would build it from joined parts; the Sinai lampstand was hammered from one ingot. The artisan who made it worked within the constraints of a single material mass, requiring extraordinary three-dimensional metalsmithing skill.
23: The seven lamps made, with their tongs and fire-pans, all of pure gold. The maintenance tools were completed to specification (Exodus 25:38). Seven lamps for the seven branches — the number of flames in YHWH's holy place equal to the number of days in the week, equal to the number of completeness. The tongs and fire-pans of pure gold for daily lamp maintenance: even routine sanctuary tasks used the most precious materials.
24: One talent of pure gold for the lampstand and all its utensils. One talent = approximately 34 kg of gold. This single weight represents the lampstand's material cost, separate from all other gold used in the tabernacle. The talent-measure confirms that the lampstand was not simply heavy but precisely heavy — its material was weighed and accounted for in the final inventory (Exodus 38:24).
25: The incense altar (mizbach hamiqtar) of acacia wood — 1 × 1 × 2 cubits — with integral horns. The incense altar's construction (following the ark, table, and lampstand) completes the inner-sanctuary furniture set. Its square, proportioned form (Exodus 1:1:2) and integral horns match the blueprint of Exodus 30:1–5. The incense altar was the daily-ministry object that came closest to the veil — positioned before the veil, between the lampstand and the table, at the threshold of the most holy place.
26: Gold overlay on its top, sides, and horns, with a golden crown border. The full gold treatment (including the horns where blood was applied) confirmed that the incense altar's holiness-grade was 'most holy' (Exodus 30:10). The golden crown on the incense altar matched those on the ark and table — the three crowns of the sanctuary's innermost furnishings, marking the complete set of crowned objects in YHWH's holy place.
27: Two gold rings below the crown on opposite sides for the carrying poles. The incense altar's transport system mirrors all other sanctuary objects: rings cast for poles. The two-ring system (rather than the four rings of the ark and table) reflects the incense altar's narrower body — two rings, one per side, sufficed for stable carrying of the smaller object.
28: Acacia-wood poles overlaid with gold for the incense altar. The standard pole construction (same as all other sanctuary objects) confirms that the incense altar was fully integrated into the tabernacle's transport system. No object was excluded from the mobility principle — every furnishing had its poles ready.
29: Bezalel made the anointing oil and the pure fragrant incense — the work of a perfumer (ma'aseh roqeach*). The perfumery skill required for the five-spice anointing oil and four-spice incense formula was a distinct craft from metalworking and weaving. Bezalel's Spirit-gifting explicitly included 'all crafts' (Exodus 31:5), encompassing this specialized work. The incense and oil sealed the sanctuary's full sensory engagement: sight (gold, colors, light), smell (incense), touch (anointing) all came from Bezalel's workshop.
